Correspondence and Network Analysis of Obesity-Related Data

TURRI Valentín

2025-05-10

1. Data

We start by reading the data. The goal is to perform a correspondence analysis to explore relationships between weight and age.

# Read CSV file
path <- "C:/Users/valen/OneDrive/Escritorio/Maestría en Ciencia de Datos/AID/TP4/obesidad.csv"
obesity_stats <- read_csv(path, locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8"))
## Rows: 20758 Columns: 18
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (9): Gender, family_history_with_overweight, FAVC, CAEC, SMOKE, SCC, CAL...
## dbl (9): id, Age, Height, Weight, FCVC, NCP, CH2O, FAF, TUE
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# Random sample of 500 cases
set.seed(920)
obesity_sample <- obesity_stats %>% sample_n(500)

str(obesity_sample)
## spc_tbl_ [500 × 18] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ id                            : num [1:500] 5801 2535 139 20289 20563 ...
##  $ Gender                        : chr [1:500] "Male" "Female" "Female" "Female" ...
##  $ Age                           : num [1:500] 21 26 19.6 33 18 ...
##  $ Height                        : num [1:500] 1.62 1.64 1.5 1.65 1.6 ...
##  $ Weight                        : num [1:500] 70 104.9 71.6 77 45 ...
##  $ family_history_with_overweight: chr [1:500] "no" "yes" "yes" "no" ...
##  $ FAVC                          : chr [1:500] "no" "yes" "no" "yes" ...
##  $ FCVC                          : num [1:500] 2 3 3 3 3 ...
##  $ NCP                           : num [1:500] 3 3 3.09 3 3 ...
##  $ CAEC                          : chr [1:500] "no" "Sometimes" "Sometimes" "Sometimes" ...
##  $ SMOKE                         : chr [1:500] "no" "no" "no" "no" ...
##  $ CH2O                          : num [1:500] 3 2.58 2 2 2 ...
##  $ SCC                           : chr [1:500] "no" "no" "no" "no" ...
##  $ FAF                           : num [1:500] 1 0 1.08 0 1 ...
##  $ TUE                           : num [1:500] 0 0.402 1 1 2 ...
##  $ CALC                          : chr [1:500] "no" "Sometimes" "no" "Sometimes" ...
##  $ MTRANS                        : chr [1:500] "Public_Transportation" "Public_Transportation" "Public_Transportation" "Automobile" ...
##  $ NObeyesdad                    : chr [1:500] "Overweight_Level_I" "Obesity_Type_III" "Overweight_Level_II" "Overweight_Level_II" ...
##  - attr(*, "spec")=
##   .. cols(
##   ..   id = col_double(),
##   ..   Gender = col_character(),
##   ..   Age = col_double(),
##   ..   Height = col_double(),
##   ..   Weight = col_double(),
##   ..   family_history_with_overweight = col_character(),
##   ..   FAVC = col_character(),
##   ..   FCVC = col_double(),
##   ..   NCP = col_double(),
##   ..   CAEC = col_character(),
##   ..   SMOKE = col_character(),
##   ..   CH2O = col_double(),
##   ..   SCC = col_character(),
##   ..   FAF = col_double(),
##   ..   TUE = col_double(),
##   ..   CALC = col_character(),
##   ..   MTRANS = col_character(),
##   ..   NObeyesdad = col_character()
##   .. )
##  - attr(*, "problems")=<externalptr>
obesity_sample <- obesity_sample %>%
  mutate(
    Weight_cat = cut(
      Weight,
      breaks = c(0, 50, 70, 90, 200),
      labels = c("Low", "Normal", "High", "Very High"),
      right = FALSE
    ),
    Age_cat = cut(
      Age,
      breaks = c(0, 18, 30, 50, 100),
      labels = c("Young", "Young Adult", "Adult", "Older"),
      right = FALSE
    )
  )

A contingency table is created.

# Create contingency table
weight_age_table <- table(obesity_sample$Weight_cat, obesity_sample$Age_cat)

# Display table
kable(weight_age_table, caption = "Contingency table: Categorized Weight vs. Categorized Age")
Contingency table: Categorized Weight vs. Categorized Age
Young Young Adult Adult Older
Low 6 31 0 0
Normal 14 87 5 0
High 7 92 33 0
Very High 3 185 37 0 
# Relative table (row proportions)
relative_table <- prop.table(weight_age_table, margin = 1)
relative_table_df <- as.data.frame(relative_table)
colnames(relative_table_df) <- c("Weight_cat", "Age_cat", "Relative_frequency")

# Bar chart
ggplot(relative_table_df, aes(x = Weight_cat, y = Relative_frequency, fill = Age_cat)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Age distribution by weight category",
    x = "Weight category",
    y = "Relative frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

A Chi-square independence test is performed to assess whether there is independence between the selected variables.

# Remove columns with all zeros (if any)
weight_age_table_filtered <- weight_age_table[, colSums(weight_age_table) > 0]

# Chi-square test (suppress warnings to avoid knit warnings, if desired)
chi_test <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(weight_age_table_filtered))

# Show p-value in scientific notation
cat(paste0("**p-value (Chi-square):** ", format(chi_test$p.value, scientific = TRUE), "<br>"))
## **p-value (Chi-square):** 1.182408e-08<br>

The p-value is very small, so the data suggest some degree of dependence.

# Simple Correspondence Analysis (CA)
ca_result <- CA(weight_age_table_filtered, graph = FALSE)

# Biplot
fviz_ca_biplot(ca_result, repel = TRUE) +
  ggtitle("Correspondence Analysis Biplot - Weight vs Age")

Interpretation (CA):

  • “Young” appears clearly separated in the upper-right quadrant, associated with “Low” and “Normal” weight categories, indicating that younger individuals tend to have lower weights.
  • “Young Adult” and “Very High” are positioned close to each other, suggesting that individuals in that age group more frequently present very high weights.
  • “Adult” appears relatively close to the “High” category, indicating some association between these two.
  • “Normal” and “Low” are fairly close, which may indicate there is no strong age differentiation among individuals in those weight categories.
  • Dimension 1 mainly separates weight levels (from “Very High” to “Low”), while Dimension 2 captures smaller differences, likely related to age extremes such as “Adult” and “Young”.
# Select only categorical columns for MCA
mca_vars <- obesity_sample %>%
  select(SMOKE, Gender, family_history_with_overweight, MTRANS)

# Display the first rows
head(mca_vars)
## # A tibble: 6 × 4
##   SMOKE Gender family_history_with_overweight MTRANS               
##   <chr> <chr>  <chr>                          <chr>                
## 1 no    Male   no                             Public_Transportation
## 2 no    Female yes                            Public_Transportation
## 3 no    Female yes                            Public_Transportation
## 4 no    Female no                             Automobile           
## 5 no    Female no                             Public_Transportation
## 6 no    Male   no                             Public_Transportation
# Apply MCA
mca_result <- MCA(mca_vars, graph = FALSE)
# Visualize MCA biplot
fviz_mca_biplot(
  mca_result,
  repel = TRUE,
  ggtheme = theme_minimal()
) +
  ggtitle("Multiple Correspondence Analysis Biplot")

# Variable contributions to Dimension 1
cat("Contributions to Dimension 1:\n")
## Contributions to Dimension 1:
print(fviz_contrib(mca_result, choice = "var", axes = 1, top = 10))

# Variable contributions to Dimension 2
cat("Contributions to Dimension 2:\n")
## Contributions to Dimension 2:
print(fviz_contrib(mca_result, choice = "var", axes = 2, top = 10))

Interpretation (MCA)

1. Multiple Correspondence Analysis Biplot
The biplot shows the projection of individuals (blue) and variable categories (red) onto the first two MCA dimensions.

2. Contribution to Dimension 1
The variables contributing the most to Dimension 1 are mainly the mode of transportation and gender. The category MTRANS_Bike stands out (around 35% contribution), indicating a strong differentiation from the rest. It is followed by MTRANS_Automobile (~18%), Gender_Male (~11%), Gender_Female (~9.5%), and MTRANS_Motorbike (~8%). This suggests Dimension 1 is strongly influenced by transportation preferences (with “Bike” as a distinctive behavior) and also by gender.

3. Contribution to Dimension 2
For Dimension 2, the most influential categories are MTRANS_Walking (~30%), followed by Gender_Male (~18%), Gender_Female (~17%), and MTRANS_Bike (~16%). This dimension seems to reflect a combination of gender and preference for more accessible/active transportation (e.g., walking). The strong loading of MTRANS_Walking suggests this dimension could be interpreted as representing preference or access to less motorized transportation, differentiating certain gender and behavior profiles.
